data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me collect all packages and their versions, then pick the highest for each:

| Package | Api | Worker | Shared | Tests | Highest |
|---------|-----|--------|--------|-------|---------|
| System.Text.Json | 10.0.1 | 8.0.4 | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 |
| Azure.Identity | 1.13.2 | - | 1.11.4 | - | 1.13.2 |
| Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting | - | 8.0.1 | - | - | 8.0.1 |


| OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting | - | 1.15.0 | - | - | 1.15.0 |
| xunit | - | - | - | 2.9.3 | 2.9.3 |

Now I need to set up centralized package management by creating a Directory.Packages.props file with all the highest versions listed, then strip out the version attributes from each project's package references.

</Project>

data.content:
Done. Here's what I did:

**Created `Directory.Packages.props`** with centralized versions (highest wins for conflicts):

| Package | Resolved Version | Conflict |
|---|---|---|
| `Azure.Identity` | **1.13.2** | 1.13.2 vs 1.11.4 |
| `System.Text.Json` | **10.0.1** | 10.0.1 vs 8.0.4 |
| `Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting` | 8.0.1 | — |
| `OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting` | 1.15.0 | — |
| `xunit` | 2.9.3 | — |

**Removed `Version` attributes** from all `<PackageReference>` entries in the 4 `.csproj` files so versions are managed centrally.
